Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
“Yes, I know all that you saw. You walked round the room several times,
and you knelt down by the body, and then you walked through and tried
the kitchen door, and then——”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
Indeed, I had wandered around the room several times, my initial focus on the obvious – the arrangement of the furniture and any signs of struggle. Then, kneeling by the body, I noted its position but failed to see the significance until Holmes pointed out how it faced towards the west window, as if in a final gaze at something beyond. It was only after he had carefully examined every door and window that I realized his method – systematic observation, no detail too small or insignificant.

“His face—his dress—didn’t you notice them?” Holmes broke in
impatiently.

“He must have left it behind,” muttered my companion. “You didn’t
happen to see or hear a cab after that?”

“There’s a half-sovereign for you,” my companion said, standing up and
taking his hat. “I am afraid, Rance, that you will never rise in the
force. That head of yours should be for use as well as ornament. You
might have gained your sergeant’s stripes last night. The man whom you
held in your hands is the man who holds the clue of this mystery, and
whom we are seeking. There is no use of arguing about it now; I tell
you that it is so. Come along, Doctor.”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
That evening, as Holmes handed me his winnings from a small wager, he turned to Rance with stern eyes, pointing out the oversight in our colleague’s judgment that night. The half-sovereign was not just a reward for our successful outing; it symbolized Holmes's acute observation and intuition. His ability to see what I had missed—how Rance had unwittingly held the key to solving the case—reminded me yet again of his unparalleled deductive skills, always perceiving more than anyone else in the room.

“I am rather in the dark still. It is true that the description of this
man tallies with your idea of the second party in this mystery. But why
should he come back to the house after leaving it? That is not the way
of criminals.”

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
“I am rather in the dark still. It is true that the description of this
man tallies with your idea of the second party in this mystery. But why
should he come back to the house after leaving it? That is not the way
of criminals.”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
Holmes stared intently at the man, his eyes piercing through the veil of mist that seemed to cloak our minds. I, on the other hand, was still mired in my own confusion, failing to grasp the significance of his return. It was Holmes’s sharp observation and ability to connect seemingly insignificant details that truly set him apart; his analysis revealed the subtle clues that eluded me, hinting at a complex narrative underlying the simple actions we observed.

“The ring, man, the ring: that was what he came back for. If we have no
other way of catching him, we can always bait our line with the ring. I
shall have him, Doctor—I’ll lay you two to one that I have him. I must
thank you for it all. I might not have gone but for you, and so have
missed the finest study I ever came across: a study in scarlet, eh? Why
shouldn’t we use a little art jargon. There’s the scarlet thread of
murder running through the colourless skein of life, and our duty is to
unravel it, and isolate it, and expose every inch of it. And now for
lunch, and then for Norman Neruda. Her attack and her bowing are
splendid. What’s that little thing of Chopin’s she plays so
magnificently: Tra-la-la-lira-lira-lay.”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
It was the ring, truly, the ring that brought him back in such a state of agitation. I had overlooked its significance initially, my mind preoccupied with more pressing matters at hand. Holmes’s keen eye for detail never ceased to amaze me; he saw beyond what was immediately apparent and found the scarlet thread leading us closer to our quarry. His methods were ever so elegant yet ruthlessly precise, always seeking to unravel the complexities of human motive through a lens of art and science alike.

Leaning back in the cab, this amateur bloodhound carolled away like a
lark while I meditated upon the many-sidedness of the human mind.

The more I thought of it the more extraordinary did my companion’s
hypothesis, that the man had been poisoned, appear. I remembered how he
had sniffed his lips, and had no doubt that he had detected something
which had given rise to the idea. Then, again, if not poison, what had
caused the man’s death, since there was neither wound nor marks of
strangulation? But, on the other hand, whose blood was that which lay
so thickly upon the floor? There were no signs of a struggle, nor had
the victim any weapon with which he might have wounded an antagonist.
As long as all these questions were unsolved, I felt that sleep would
be no easy matter, either for Holmes or myself. His quiet
self-confident manner convinced me that he had already formed a theory
which explained all the facts, though what it was I could not for an
instant conjecture.

Holmes's ability to see beyond the obvious and form theories based on subtle clues remains one of the most striking aspects of our partnership, revealing once again just how much more acute his mind was compared to my own.

It was only later, as I recalled how he had paused to smell the air around the body, that I realized his keen sense of detection was at work. Holmes's ability to see beyond the obvious and form theories based on subtle clues remains one of the most striking aspects of our partnership, revealing once again just how much more acute his mind was compared to my own.

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
The more I thought of it the more extraordinary did my companion’s
hypothesis, that the man had been poisoned, appear. I remembered how he
had sniffed his lips, and had no doubt that he had detected something
which had given rise to the idea. Then, again, if not poison, what had
caused the man’s death, since there was neither wound nor marks of
strangulation? But, on the other hand, whose blood was that which lay
so thickly upon the floor? There were no signs of a struggle, nor had
the victim any weapon with which he might have wounded an antagonist.
As long as all these questions were unsolved, I felt that sleep would
be no easy matter, either for Holmes or myself. His quiet
self-confident manner convinced me that he had already formed a theory
which explained all the facts, though what it was I could not for an
instant conjecture.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: Having closely examined the scene of the incident, I observed that my companion had noted a peculiar odor upon the victim’s lips, which he suspected to be an indicator of poison. This inference was further supported by the absence of any physical signs of struggle or defensive wounds, which suggested that the death was not due to violence but rather to some other means that could incapacitate and claim life silently. The thick bloodstains on the floor also pointed towards a sudden, non-violent demise, likely as a result of poisoning.

Moreover, the lack of any weapon at the scene indicated that the killer had either been adept in administering the poison without leaving evidence or that no such means were involved in the death. This absence of tools and signs of conflict corroborated my own suspicion of a sudden, unexpected end due to some form of intoxication or poisoning.

Thus, through these observations and their logical implications, I concluded that the man had indeed been poisoned; the odor on his lips, the bloodstains, and the lack of struggle all pointed to this as the most plausible cause of death.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):

5. Despite these questions remaining unsolved, Holmes exhibited quiet self-confidence and had already formed a theory explaining all facts.

7. The companion found the hypothesis of poisoning extraordinary but plausible based on his actions.
8. There is no evidence of murder by force or weapon, suggesting an alternative method for the victim's death.
9. The absence of struggle suggests a non-physical form of incapacitation leading to death.
10. Holmes’s confident demeanor implies he has solved the mystery in his mind.

CONCLUSION: Holmes has inferred that the victim was likely poisoned and not killed through physical violence, despite no obvious signs of poison being present at first glance.

yourself. This Brixton Road affair has upset you.”

It was on a chilly evening, and the fog lay thick in the air like a shroud. I had been pacing restlessly, my mind troubled by the recent events that seemed to twist and turn in unpredictable ways. Sherlock, as always, noticed my discomfort before I did myself, his eyes piercing through the mist as if they could see straight into the heart of the matter. His observation that one must view the world as expansively as Nature itself struck me then, and it still resonates—his method, so keen and unforgiving in its pursuit of truth, was a testament to the breadth he demanded from his own perceptions.

seen the evening paper?”

In retrospect, I must admit my initial confusion; how could such an insignificant detail escape my notice? Yet there it lay, the fallen ring, unnoticed by me until Sherlock Holmes pointed it out. “Oh yes, you have,” said he, handing me one. “This will do very well.
It is almost a facsimile.”

“Why, the man in the brown coat—our florid friend with the square toes.
If he does not come himself he will send an accomplice.”

“Not at all. If my view of the case is correct, and I have every reason
to believe that it is, this man would rather risk anything than lose
the ring. According to my notion he dropped it while stooping over
Drebber’s body, and did not miss it at the time. After leaving the
house he discovered his loss and hurried back, but found the police
already in possession, owing to his own folly in leaving the candle
burning. He had to pretend to be drunk in order to allay the suspicions
which might have been aroused by his appearance at the gate. Now put
yourself in that man’s place. On thinking the matter over, it must have
occurred to him that it was possible that he had lost the ring in the
road after leaving the house. What would he do, then? He would eagerly
look out for the evening papers in the hope of seeing it among the
articles found. His eye, of course, would light upon this. He would be
overjoyed. Why should he fear a trap? There would be no reason in his
eyes why the finding of the ring should be connected with the murder.
He would come. He will come. You shall see him within an hour?”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The gentleman, Drebber, upon realizing his loss of the ring immediately sought to return home, yet was deterred by the presence of the police. His hasty departure and subsequent attempt to feign intoxication indicate a heightened sense of guilt or fear. Moreover, the detective infers that Drebber would logically consider the possibility of having dropped the ring after leaving the house but before being seen at the gate. In his estimation, upon reading the newspaper that evening, Drebber would likely discover information about the ring’s location and consequently seek to reclaim it, thus confirming the detective's prediction.

already in possession, owing to his own folly in leaving the candle
burning. He had to pretend to be drunk in order to allay the suspicions
which might have been aroused by his appearance at the gate. Now put
yourself in that man’s place. On thinking the matter over, it must have
occurred to him that it was possible that he had lost the ring in the
road after leaving the house. What would he do, then? He would eagerly
look out for the evening papers in the hope of seeing it among the
articles found. His eye, of course, would light upon this. He would be
overjoyed. Why should he fear a trap? There would be no reason in his
eyes why the finding of the ring should be connected with the murder.
He would come. He will come. You shall see him within an hour?”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The gentleman, Drebber, upon realizing his loss of the ring immediately sought to return home, yet was deterred by the presence of the police. His hasty departure and subsequent attempt to feign intoxication indicate a heightened sense of guilt or fear. Moreover, the detective infers that Drebber would logically consider the possibility of having dropped the ring after leaving the house but before being seen at the gate. In his estimation, upon reading the newspaper that evening, Drebber would likely discover information about the ring’s location and consequently seek to reclaim it, thus confirming the detective's prediction.

In that dimly lit room, the air thick with tension, Holmes stood by the window, his eyes fixed on something beyond the foggy streets. It was a routine request, but in his hands, the revolver took on new significance—Holmes knew more than he let on. As I loaded and cleaned the weapon, I felt my initial dismissiveness toward such preparations. Yet, it was Holmes's unwavering attention to detail that always saved us, even when I saw only shadows where he perceived danger.

head was still firm on his shoulders when this little brown-backed
volume was struck off.”

With many mumbled blessings and protestations of gratitude the old
crone packed it away in her pocket, and shuffled off down the stairs.
Sherlock Holmes sprang to his feet the moment that she was gone and
rushed into his room. He returned in a few seconds enveloped in an
ulster and a cravat. “I’ll follow her,” he said, hurriedly; “she must
be an accomplice, and will lead me to him. Wait up for me.” The hall
door had hardly slammed behind our visitor before Holmes had descended
the stair. Looking through the window I could see her walking feebly
along the other side, while her pursuer dogged her some little distance
behind. “Either his whole theory is incorrect,” I thought to myself,
“or else he will be led now to the heart of the mystery.” There was no
need for him to ask me to wait up for him, for I felt that sleep was
impossible until I heard the result of his adventure.

His quick actions always astounded me—how he could deduce the presence of an accomplice from a mere glance at that woman’s exit. Watching him follow, I couldn’t help but wonder if my observations were as keen as his.

“Old woman be damned!” said Sherlock Holmes, sharply. “We were the old
women to be so taken in. It must have been a young man, and an active
one, too, besides being an incomparable actor. The get-up was
inimitable. He saw that he was followed, no doubt, and used this means
of giving me the slip. It shows that the man we are after is not as
lonely as I imagined he was, but has friends who are ready to risk
something for him. Now, Doctor, you are looking done-up. Take my advice
and turn in.”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The young man, therefore, must have been cunningly disguised to elude detection; his agility was evident from his ability to escape swiftly. The manner in which he contrived his appearance, coupled with the meticulous attention to detail that ensured he went undetected by me until it suited him, indicated a rare talent for deception and secrecy. Moreover, the fact that he had managed to outwit not only myself but also what appeared to be a solitary situation suggested a support network of complicit individuals who were willing to aid him in his endeavors, thus negating my initial assumption of solitude. These observations collectively lead me to deduce that the perpetrator is neither isolated nor as vulnerable as initially perceived, implying a more complex web of intrigue and cooperation than I had originally considered.

The _Daily Telegraph_ remarked that in the history of crime there had
seldom been a tragedy which presented stranger features. The German
name of the victim, the absence of all other motive, and the sinister
inscription on the wall, all pointed to its perpetration by political
refugees and revolutionists. The Socialists had many branches in
America, and the deceased had, no doubt, infringed their unwritten

Yet, it was Holmes who saw through this complexity, revealing that the key lay not in the obscure references but in the meticulous examination of the crime scene—a lesson I would come to appreciate more with each passing day.

the acuteness and energy of Mr. Gregson of Scotland Yard.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: From the evidence at hand, it is clear that Drebber was assaulted by a assailant wielding a stick, which struck him in the vicinity of his stomach, an area where such a blow could incapacitate or result in fatal consequences without leaving a discernible wound. The timing and location of this incident on the Brixton Road suggest that Charpentier, with whom Drebber was last seen, had motive to remove the body from public view during the inclement weather; indeed, the lack of witnesses aligns with his opportunity for committing such an act unobserved. The subsequent discovery of a candle, bloodstains, and the peculiar writing on the wall within the abandoned premises indicates that Charpentier, in a bid to mislead investigators, created a false narrative involving potential murder weapons and a note left behind to distract from his true motives. Thus, it is reasonable to infer that Drebber was murdered by someone known to him who had access to the scene, with Charpentier being complicit in disposing of the body to cover up his involvement.

“I flatter myself that I have managed it rather neatly,” the detective
answered proudly. “The young man volunteered a statement, in which he
said that after following Drebber some time, the latter perceived him,
and took a cab in order to get away from him. On his way home he met an
old shipmate, and took a long walk with him. On being asked where this
old shipmate lived, he was unable to give any satisfactory reply. I
think the whole case fits together uncommonly well. What amuses me is
to think of Lestrade, who had started off upon the wrong scent. I am
afraid he won’t make much of it. Why, by Jove, here’s the very man
himself!”

Holmes' method always relied on tiny details overlooked by others, and in this instance, it was the lack of specific direction given for the old shipmate's address that led us straight to the solution.

“I have no objection,” Lestrade answered, seating himself. “I freely
confess that I was of the opinion that Stangerson was concerned in the
death of Drebber. This fresh development has shown me that I was
completely mistaken. Full of the one idea, I set myself to find out
what had become of the Secretary. They had been seen together at Euston
Station about half-past eight on the evening of the third. At two in
the morning Drebber had been found in the Brixton Road. The question
which confronted me was to find out how Stangerson had been employed
between 8.30 and the time of the crime, and what had become of him
afterwards. I telegraphed to Liverpool, giving a description of the
man, and warning them to keep a watch upon the American boats. I then
set to work calling upon all the hotels and lodging-houses in the
vicinity of Euston. You see, I argued that if Drebber and his companion
had become separated, the natural course for the latter would be to put
up somewhere in the vicinity for the night, and then to hang about the
station again next morning.”

Holmes sat quietly, observing Lestrade intently as he spoke. At first, I was caught up in Lestrade’s narrative, focusing on his initial assumptions and the methods he employed to verify them. However, Holmes saw through the surface of the case, recognizing the inconsistencies that hinted at a deeper mystery. His keen eye for detail and logical deduction revealed how Stangerson's movements could not be accounted for by simple separation; it was this subtle insight that set our investigation on a more promising path.

“They would be likely to agree on some meeting-place beforehand,”
remarked Holmes.

“It seemed to me that my sudden appearance might shake his nerves and
lead him to say something unguarded. The Boots volunteered to show me
the room: it was on the second floor, and there was a small corridor
leading up to it. The Boots pointed out the door to me, and was about
to go downstairs again when I saw something that made me feel sickish,
in spite of my twenty years’ experience. From under the door there
curled a little red ribbon of blood, which had meandered across the
passage and formed a little pool along the skirting at the other side.
I gave a cry, which brought the Boots back. He nearly fainted when he
saw it. The door was locked on the inside, but we put our shoulders to
it, and knocked it in. The window of the room was open, and beside the
window, all huddled up, lay the body of a man in his nightdress. He was
quite dead, and had been for some time, for his limbs were rigid and
cold. When we turned him over, the Boots recognized him at once as
being the same gentleman who had engaged the room under the name of
Joseph Stangerson. The cause of death was a deep stab in the left side,
which must have penetrated the heart. And now comes the strangest part
of the affair. What do you suppose was above the murdered man?”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The presence of blood beneath the door suggests a struggle or attempt to conceal the body. The huddled position of the deceased gentleman indicates a swift and sudden demise, not consistent with natural causes. The stab wound in the heart points towards murder committed by someone who had access to the room but was forced to leave hastily, as evidenced by the open window. Most telling is the question posed at the end: "What do you suppose was above the murdered man?" This implies there could be evidence or a clue directly overhead, possibly a footprint or a discarded object, that would provide insight into the murderer's identity and actions—a deduction further reinforced by the locked door, suggesting an attempt to prevent discovery.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. The room is on the second floor and has a small corridor leading up to it.
2. There is a red ribbon of blood under the door that had meandered across the passage and formed a pool at the other side.
3. The door was locked from the inside.
4. The window of the room was open.
5. A man in his nightdress, identified as Joseph Stangerson, lay dead with rigid and cold limbs on the floor beside the window.
6. He had suffered a deep stab wound to the left side that must have penetrated the heart.
7. The Boots recognized the body as belonging to Mr. Stangerson.
8. There is something strange about what was above the murdered man.

1. The detective's appearance caused the murderer to act impulsively, leading to his death.
2. The blood trail suggests a struggle or that the victim tried to flee.
3. The locked door from the inside indicates an attempt to prevent discovery but also implies the killer could not escape easily.
4. The open window might suggest an exit route or a motive related to ventilation or weather conditions.
5. The huddled position of the body suggests a violent death and possibly a struggle for dominance.
6. The precise stab wound location points to a calculated attack, targeting a vital area.
7. The identity of the victim is confirmed by the Boots.
8. The strangest part of the affair refers to what was above Mr. Stangerson's body.

CONCLUSION: A murder has occurred in a room on the second floor, where the door was locked from the inside and blood led to an open window. The victim, Joseph Stangerson, died from a stab wound that punctured his heart, leading to his death after a struggle indicated by the state of the body. The presence above Mr. Stangerson's body suggests a possible connection or clue related to the crime but requires further investigation.

curled a little red ribbon of blood, which had meandered across the
passage and formed a little pool along the skirting at the other side.
I gave a cry, which brought the Boots back. He nearly fainted when he
saw it. The door was locked on the inside, but we put our shoulders to
it, and knocked it in. The window of the room was open, and beside the
window, all huddled up, lay the body of a man in his nightdress. He was
quite dead, and had been for some time, for his limbs were rigid and
cold. When we turned him over, the Boots recognized him at once as
being the same gentleman who had engaged the room under the name of
Joseph Stangerson. The cause of death was a deep stab in the left side,
which must have penetrated the heart. And now comes the strangest part
of the affair. What do you suppose was above the murdered man?”
“The word RACHE, written in letters of blood,” he said.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: From the absence of any other items save a single telegram in Drebber’s possession, I infer that neither robbery nor the theft of documents motivated his murder. The fact that Stangerson carried the purse consistently suggests a complicity or familiarity not rooted in financial gain, but rather an understanding or shared purpose that extends beyond mere pecuniary transactions. The telegram itself hints at a connection to an individual named "J.H." who was known to be abroad, which further complicates any straightforward narrative of robbery or personal enmity as the driving forces behind Drebber’s tragic end. Thus, I deduce that this case involves elements of a more complex and possibly international intrigue, where financial transactions and personal correspondence serve as mere veils for deeper, perhaps hidden, motives.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. Stangerson had Drebber’s purse in his pocket.

3. The purse contained approximately eighty pounds.
4. Nothing was taken from the purse.
5. Robbery does not appear to be a motive for these crimes.
6. There were no papers or memoranda in Drebber’s pocket, aside from a single telegram.
7. The telegram is dated from Cleveland about a month ago.
8. The telegram states 'J. H. is in Europe'.
9. No name is appended to the message.

Inferences:
1. Stangerson may have a routine or familiarity with handling Drebber’s financial matters.
2. The presence of money and lack of theft suggest either an internal crime where the murderer had access to the purse, or that no robbery occurred.
3. The absence of other documents in Drebber’s pocket implies he was not carrying important papers at the time of his death.
4. The telegram is likely relevant to understanding who J. H. might be and his relation to Drebber.

Conclusion: 
The evidence suggests an internal crime where no money was taken, pointing towards a possible personal dispute or other motives that are yet unknown, as indicated by the unusual nature of the crimes combined with the presence of Stangerson’s usual financial handling role. The telegram about J. H. being in Europe is a key piece of information that needs further investigation to understand its significance.

upon those pills?”

“It can’t be a coincidence,” he cried, at last springing from his chair
and pacing wildly up and down the room; “it is impossible that it
should be a mere coincidence. The very pills which I suspected in the
case of Drebber are actually found after the death of Stangerson. And
yet they are inert. What can it mean? Surely my whole chain of
reasoning cannot have been false. It is impossible! And yet this
wretched dog is none the worse. Ah, I have it! I have it!” With a
perfect shriek of delight he rushed to the box, cut the other pill in
two, dissolved it, added milk, and presented it to the terrier. The
unfortunate creature’s tongue seemed hardly to have been moistened in
it before it gave a convulsive shiver in every limb, and lay as rigid
and lifeless as if it had been struck by lightning.

and pacing wildly up and down the room; “it is impossible that it
should be a mere coincidence. The very pills which I suspected in the
case of Drebber are actually found after the death of Stangerson. And
yet they are inert. What can it mean? Surely my whole chain of
reasoning cannot have been false. It is impossible! And yet this
wretched dog is none the worse. Ah, I have it! I have it!” With a
perfect shriek of delight he rushed to the box, cut the other pill in
two, dissolved it, added milk, and presented it to the terrier. The
unfortunate creature’s tongue seemed hardly to have been moistened in
it before it gave a convulsive shiver in every limb, and lay as rigid
and lifeless as if it had been struck by lightning.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The presence of identical pills in both Drebber and Stangerson’s cases cannot be a mere coincidence; hence, they must serve a common purpose. Upon witnessing the terrier’s life-threatening reaction to the second pill, it is clear that these pills are indeed active agents, not inert as initially suspected. The detective deduces that the substance within the pills is likely a poison used by someone to eliminate Drebber and Stangerson, and his reasoning is confirmed by the terrier’s swift and dramatic response, which parallels the fatal effects observed in the humans.

Sherlock Holmes drew a long breath, and wiped the perspiration from his
forehead. “I should have more faith,” he said; “I ought to know by this
time that when a fact appears to be opposed to a long train of
deductions, it invariably proves to be capable of bearing some other
interpretation. Of the two pills in that box one was of the most deadly
poison, and the other was entirely harmless. I ought to have known that
before ever I saw the box at all.”

PASSAGE:
Sherlock Holmes drew a long breath, and wiped the perspiration from his
forehead. “I should have more faith,” he said; “I ought to know by this
time that when a fact appears to be opposed to a long train of

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The presence of a single deadly pill amidst innocuous ones within the same container is a phenomenon I have observed elsewhere - a means to mislead the unwary. The fact that both pills were in identical containers speaks volumes; it is a stratagem, designed to obscure the truth. The perspiration on my forehead bears witness to the strain of deduction, but also confirms my growing conviction that the harmless pill's appearance was merely a ruse. Thus, I conclude that whatever appears evident at first must be subjected to rigorous scrutiny - for in this case, it is the very absence of immediate danger which hides the real peril.

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
This last statement appeared to me to be so startling, that I could
hardly believe that he was in his sober senses. There was the dead dog,
however, to prove that his conjecture had been correct. It seemed to me
that the mists in my own mind were gradually clearing away, and I began
to have a dim, vague perception of the truth.

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
It was then, upon reflection, that I finally grasped the truth behind Holmes’s theory about the dead dog. At first, his conjecture had seemed almost preposterous to me, but as I stood there, staring at the evidence, a clarity began to emerge in my mind. Holmes's method, always so brilliant and precise, was once again revealed to me in its full glory, as he dissected our predicament with such surgical precision that even I could not help but be awestruck by his analytical prowess.

“All this seems strange to you,” continued Holmes, “because you failed
at the beginning of the inquiry to grasp the importance of the single
real clue which was presented to you. I had the good fortune to seize
upon that, and everything which has occurred since then has served to
confirm my original supposition, and, indeed, was the logical sequence
of it. Hence things which have perplexed you and made the case more
obscure, have served to enlighten me and to strengthen my conclusions.
It is a mistake to confound strangeness with mystery. The most
commonplace crime is often the most mysterious because it presents no
new or special features from which deductions may be drawn. This murder
would have been infinitely more difficult to unravel had the body of
the victim been simply found lying in the roadway without any of those
_outré_ and sensational accompaniments which have rendered it
remarkable. These strange details, far from making the case more
difficult, have really had the effect of making it less so.”

Mr. Gregson, who had listened to this address with considerable
impatience, could contain himself no longer. “Look here, Mr. Sherlock
Holmes,” he said, “we are all ready to acknowledge that you are a smart
man, and that you have your own methods of working. We want something
more than mere theory and preaching now, though. It is a case of taking
the man. I have made my case out, and it seems I was wrong. Young
Charpentier could not have been engaged in this second affair. Lestrade
went after his man, Stangerson, and it appears that he was wrong too.
You have thrown out hints here, and hints there, and seem to know more
than we do, but the time has come when we feel that we have a right to
ask you straight how much you do know of the business. Can you name the
man who did it?”

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:

Holmes,” he said, “we are all ready to acknowledge that you are a smart
man, and that you have your own methods of working. We want something
more than mere theory and preaching now, though. It is a case of taking
the man. I have made my case out, and it seems I was wrong. Young
Charpentier could not have been engaged in this second affair. Lestrade
went after his man, Stangerson, and it appears that he was wrong too.
You have thrown out hints here, and hints there, and seem to know more
than we do, but the time has come when we feel that we have a right to
ask you straight how much you do know of the business. Can you name the
man who did it?”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
Holmes stood there with an air of serene confidence, his eyes piercing through Gregson's impatience. I, on the other hand, was initially taken aback by the directness and pressure from our colleagues; their frustration seemed to cloud my judgment, making me less perceptive than usual. Yet, Holmes's method always shone through, as he calmly invited them to question him further, knowing that the truth would emerge from a thorough examination of all clues—his subtle hints were not mere theories but carefully placed seeds of understanding.

“There will be no more murders,” he said at last, stopping abruptly and
facing us. “You can put that consideration out of the question. You
have asked me if I know the name of the assassin. I do. The mere
knowing of his name is a small thing, however, compared with the power
of laying our hands upon him. This I expect very shortly to do. I have
good hopes of managing it through my own arrangements; but it is a
thing which needs delicate handling, for we have a shrewd and desperate
man to deal with, who is supported, as I have had occasion to prove, by
another who is as clever as himself. As long as this man has no idea
that anyone can have a clue there is some chance of securing him; but
if he had the slightest suspicion, he would change his name, and vanish
in an instant among the four million inhabitants of this great city.
Without meaning to hurt either of your feelings, I am bound to say that
I consider these men to be more than a match for the official force,
and that is why I have not asked your assistance. If I fail I shall, of
course, incur all the blame due to this omission; but that I am
prepared for. At present I am ready to promise that the instant that I
can communicate with you without endangering my own combinations, I
shall do so.”

When he turned the corner, he came full in sight of the spot where the
fire had been lit. There was still a glowing pile of wood ashes there,
but it had evidently not been tended since his departure. The same dead
silence still reigned all round. With his fears all changed to
convictions, he hurried on. There was no living creature near the
remains of the fire: animals, man, maiden, all were gone. It was only
too clear that some sudden and terrible disaster had occurred during
his absence—a disaster which had embraced them all, and yet had left no
traces behind it.

silence still reigned all round. With his fears all changed to
convictions, he hurried on. There was no living creature near the
remains of the fire: animals, man, maiden, all were gone. It was only
too clear that some sudden and terrible disaster had occurred during
his absence—a disaster which had embraced them all, and yet had left no
traces behind it.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. A glowing pile of wood ashes remains at a fire site.
2. The fire has not been tended since someone's departure.
3. There is dead silence around the area.
4. Fears have turned into convictions about a disaster.
5. No living creatures are near the fire remains.
6. The absence of traces suggests a sudden and terrible event that affected everyone.
INFERENCES:
1. Someone was recently at the fire site but has since left.
2. The site is currently unattended, suggesting an urgency to leave quickly.
3. The silence and the absence of people indicate a dramatic event possibly involving others in the vicinity.
4. The event resulted in a rapid evacuation or disappearance of all individuals present.
5. There are no physical signs pointing to the nature of the disaster.
CONCLUSION: A significant and sudden disaster occurred at the fire site, leading to an immediate evacuation of everyone involved without leaving any obvious clues as to its cause.

CONCLUSION: Some sudden and terrible disaster had befallen everyone, leaving no traces.

“They were very cunning, though. They must have thought that there was

“The moment for which I had waited so long had at last come. I had my
enemies within my power. Together they could protect each other, but
singly they were at my mercy. I did not act, however, with undue
precipitation. My plans were already formed. There is no satisfaction
in vengeance unless the offender has time to realize who it is that
strikes him, and why retribution has come upon him. I had my plans
arranged by which I should have the opportunity of making the man who
had wronged me understand that his old sin had found him out. It
chanced that some days before a gentleman who had been engaged in
looking over some houses in the Brixton Road had dropped the key of one
of them in my carriage. It was claimed that same evening, and returned;
but in the interval I had taken a moulding of it, and had a duplicate
constructed. By means of this I had access to at least one spot in this
great city where I could rely upon being free from interruption. How to
get Drebber to that house was the difficult problem which I had now to
solve.

“He walked down the road and went into one or two liquor shops, staying
for nearly half-an-hour in the last of them. When he came out he
staggered in his walk, and was evidently pretty well on. There was a
hansom just in front of me, and he hailed it. I followed it so close
that the nose of my horse was within a yard of his driver the whole
way. We rattled across Waterloo Bridge and through miles of streets,
until, to my astonishment, we found ourselves back in the Terrace in
which he had boarded. I could not imagine what his intention was in
returning there; but I went on and pulled up my cab a hundred yards or
so from the house. He entered it, and his hansom drove away. Give me a
glass of water, if you please. My mouth gets dry with the talking.”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: Upon observing the chaotic yet methodical nature of the crime scene, I deduced that the perpetrator was someone who had a routine but deviated from it to commit the act, suggesting a familiarity with the environment and thus limiting potential suspects. The specific placement of the victim’s belongings indicated a hasty departure, which inferred panic or fear; this led me to conclude the culprit was likely someone who held an emotional attachment or personal grievance against the victim. Additionally, subtle inconsistencies in the alibi provided by several individuals hinted at a lie, narrowing down the list further. By combining these observations with the physical evidence left behind—such as a torn piece of fabric and an unusual ink mark—I systematically eliminated possibilities until only one profile matched all criteria. Thus, through a series of logical deductions, I was able to identify the individual responsible for the crime within the allotted timeframe.

“I have already explained to you that what is out of the common is
usually a guide rather than a hindrance. In solving a problem of this
sort, the grand thing is to be able to reason backwards. That is a very
useful accomplishment, and a very easy one, but people do not practise
it much. In the every-day affairs of life it is more useful to reason
forwards, and so the other comes to be neglected. There are fifty who
can reason synthetically for one who can reason analytically.”

“I hardly expected that you would. Let me see if I can make it clearer.
Most people, if you describe a train of events to them, will tell you
what the result would be. They can put those events together in their
minds, and argue from them that something will come to pass. There are
few people, however, who, if you told them a result, would be able to
evolve from their own inner consciousness what the steps were which led
up to that result. This power is what I mean when I talk of reasoning
backwards, or analytically.”

“Now this was a case in which you were given the result and had to find
everything else for yourself. Now let me endeavour to show you the
different steps in my reasoning. To begin at the beginning. I
approached the house, as you know, on foot, and with my mind entirely
free from all impressions. I naturally began by examining the roadway,
and there, as I have already explained to you, I saw clearly the marks
of a cab, which, I ascertained by inquiry, must have been there during
the night. I satisfied myself that it was a cab and not a private
carriage by the narrow gauge of the wheels. The ordinary London growler
is considerably less wide than a gentleman’s brougham.

“This was the first point gained. I then walked slowly down the garden
path, which happened to be composed of a clay soil, peculiarly suitable
for taking impressions. No doubt it appeared to you to be a mere
trampled line of slush, but to my trained eyes every mark upon its
surface had a meaning. There is no branch of detective science which is
so important and so much neglected as the art of tracing footsteps.
Happily, I have always laid great stress upon it, and much practice has
made it second nature to me. I saw the heavy footmarks of the
constables, but I saw also the track of the two men who had first
passed through the garden. It was easy to tell that they had been
before the others, because in places their marks had been entirely
obliterated by the others coming upon the top of them. In this way my
second link was formed, which told me that the nocturnal visitors were
two in number, one remarkable for his height (as I calculated from the
length of his stride), and the other fashionably dressed, to judge from
the small and elegant impression left by his boots.

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
It was at Baskerville Hall that Holmes first demonstrated his unparalleled skill in tracing footsteps. At the time, I merely saw a muddy path worn down by the trampling of many feet, but to Sherlock Holmes, it was like looking through a new lens, revealing a world of evidence. He pointed out how the heavier prints of the constables had covered earlier and more subtle impressions left behind by two visitors—a tall man with wide strides and one who wore fashionable boots. This insight not only revealed their numbers but also hinted at their individual characteristics, showcasing Holmes's meticulous attention to detail and his ability to draw conclusions from even the most mundane observations.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: From the impressions in the garden path, it is evident that there were two intruders, one of significant height from the length of his stride, and another who was neatly attired as evidenced by the precise, smaller footprint. Thus, not only do I now have a clear picture of the physical attributes and attire of these individuals, but also their likely relationship to each other, as one appears dominant or leading by virtue of his size and the resultant path upon which the smaller man’s prints were partially erased.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. The garden path is composed of clay soil that can take impressions.
2. There are heavy footmarks made by constables.
3. There are also distinct marks from two men who passed through before the constables.
4. The earlier men's footprints were partially covered by those of the constables, indicating they arrived first.
5. One man was taller than the other as deduced from stride length differences.
6. The fashionably dressed man left small and elegant boot prints.
7. There were two nocturnal visitors who entered the property.

INFERENCES:
1. The path's clay composition aids in preserving footprints.
2. The constables arrived later than the two men they found tracks of.
3. The difference in stride length between the two individuals can be used to infer their height discrepancies.
4. The boot size and style suggest one visitor was dressed casually while the other was elegantly.

CONCLUSION: 
The detective concludes that there were at least two nocturnal visitors who entered through the garden, with one being significantly taller than the other and the latter wearing fashionable boots, while constables arrived later, their footprints overlying those of the original visitors.

“On entering the house this last inference was confirmed. My
well-booted man lay before me. The tall one, then, had done the murder,
if murder there was. There was no wound upon the dead man’s person, but
the agitated expression upon his face assured me that he had foreseen
his fate before it came upon him. Men who die from heart disease, or
any sudden natural cause, never by any chance exhibit agitation upon
their features. Having sniffed the dead man’s lips I detected a
slightly sour smell, and I came to the conclusion that he had had
poison forced upon him. Again, I argued that it had been forced upon
him from the hatred and fear expressed upon his face. By the method of
exclusion, I had arrived at this result, for no other hypothesis would
meet the facts. Do not imagine that it was a very unheard of idea. The
forcible administration of poison is by no means a new thing in
criminal annals. The cases of Dolsky in Odessa, and of Leturier in
Montpellier, will occur at once to any toxicologist.

Sniffing the lips and detecting a faintly sour smell led me to infer that poison was administered; this is corroborated by the lack of any physical struggle or injury as one would expect from a violent confrontation. This method is not uncommon in criminal history, as evidenced by historical cases such as Dolsky in Odessa and Leturier in Montpellier, which are well-known to toxicologists.

“And now came the great question as to the reason why. Robbery had not
been the object of the murder, for nothing was taken. Was it politics,
then, or was it a woman? That was the question which confronted me. I
was inclined from the first to the latter supposition. Political
assassins are only too glad to do their work and to fly. This murder
had, on the contrary, been done most deliberately, and the perpetrator
had left his tracks all over the room, showing that he had been there
all the time. It must have been a private wrong, and not a political
one, which called for such a methodical revenge. When the inscription
was discovered upon the wall I was more inclined than ever to my
opinion. The thing was too evidently a blind. When the ring was found,
however, it settled the question. Clearly the murderer had used it to
remind his victim of some dead or absent woman. It was at this point
that I asked Gregson whether he had enquired in his telegram to
Cleveland as to any particular point in Mr. Drebber’s former career. He
answered, you remember, in the negative.

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
I stood there, my mind racing through possibilities, but it was Holmes who immediately noted the deliberate nature of the crime and the presence of muddy footprints, suggesting a personal rather than political motive. When he saw me considering the affair from a romantic angle, he gently corrected my assumption with his keen observation that the ring left behind pointed to a private grudge rather than any public conflict. This case, much like many others, revealed Holmes's unparalleled ability to dissect a crime scene and identify the most likely scenario by focusing on the details I often overlooked.

been the object of the murder, for nothing was taken. Was it politics,
then, or was it a woman? That was the question which confronted me. I
was inclined from the first to the latter supposition. Political
assassins are only too glad to do their work and to fly. This murder
had, on the contrary, been done most deliberately, and the perpetrator
had left his tracks all over the room, showing that he had been there
all the time. It must have been a private wrong, and not a political
one, which called for such a methodical revenge. When the inscription
was discovered upon the wall I was more inclined than ever to my
opinion. The thing was too evidently a blind. When the ring was found,
however, it settled the question. Clearly the murderer had used it to
remind his victim of some dead or absent woman. It was at this point
that I asked Gregson whether he had enquired in his telegram to
Cleveland as to any particular point in Mr. Drebber’s former career. He
answered, you remember, in the negative.

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: The deliberate nature of the crime, coupled with the murderer’s careless handling of evidence, such as leaving tracks throughout the room, suggested a personal grievance rather than political motives. The inscription on the wall was indeed a blind, designed to mislead investigators away from the real issue at hand. However, it was the discovery of the ring that conclusively pointed towards the motive: it bore a message or an image associated with a woman, indicating that the murderer sought retribution for some wrong done by Mr. Drebber against his deceased or absent paramour. Therefore, I deduced that this murder was not politically motivated but rather a private matter of revenge, likely stemming from a past romantic entanglement between Mr. Drebber and the woman whose image the ring bore.

“I then proceeded to make a careful examination of the room, which
confirmed me in my opinion as to the murderer’s height, and furnished
me with the additional details as to the Trichinopoly cigar and the
length of his nails. I had already come to the conclusion, since there
were no signs of a struggle, that the blood which covered the floor had
burst from the murderer’s nose in his excitement. I could perceive that
the track of blood coincided with the track of his feet. It is seldom
that any man, unless he is very full-blooded, breaks out in this way
through emotion, so I hazarded the opinion that the criminal was
probably a robust and ruddy-faced man. Events proved that I had judged
correctly.

length of his nails. I had already come to the conclusion, since there
were no signs of a struggle, that the blood which covered the floor had
burst from the murderer’s nose in his excitement. I could perceive that
the track of blood coincided with the track of his feet. It is seldom
that any man, unless he is very full-blooded, breaks out in this way
through emotion, so I hazarded the opinion that the criminal was
probably a robust and ruddy-faced man. Events proved that I had judged
correctly.

CONCLUSION: The murderer was probably a robust and ruddy-faced man.

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story. Read it carefully, then complete the structured analysis.

PASSAGE:
“Having left the house, I proceeded to do what Gregson had neglected. I

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. The narrator proceeded to conduct a telegraphic inquiry after leaving the house.
2. Gregson had not performed this same inquiry.
3. The telegram was sent to the chief of police in Cleveland.
4. The inquiry focused on Enoch Drebber's marriage and any legal protection requests he had made.
5. The reply stated that Drebber sought protection from Jefferson Hope, an old love rival.
6. It also noted that Jefferson Hope was currently in Europe.
7. The narrator concluded this information provided a key to solving the mystery.
8. The narrator believed capturing the murderer now depended on securing the perpetrator.

1. The narrator acted upon their own initiative to gather additional information compared to Gregson's approach.
2. Information about Drebber's interaction with Hope through a legal request for protection suggests a past conflict between them.
3. Jefferson Hope being in Europe implies he is not currently within reach, complicating immediate apprehension.
4. The narrator deduced that the murder had some connection to the Drebber-Hope conflict.
5. The clues point towards Hope as the likely culprit or at least related to the crime.

CONCLUSION: The final conclusion is that Jefferson Hope, an old rival of Enoch Drebber, who is now in Europe, holds the key to solving the mystery and appears to be a prime suspect for the murder.

“I had already determined in my own mind that the man who had walked
into the house with Drebber, was none other than the man who had driven
the cab. The marks in the road showed me that the horse had wandered on
in a way which would have been impossible had there been anyone in
charge of it. Where, then, could the driver be, unless he were inside
the house? Again, it is absurd to suppose that any sane man would carry
out a deliberate crime under the very eyes, as it were, of a third
person, who was sure to betray him. Lastly, supposing one man wished to
dog another through London, what better means could he adopt than to
turn cabdriver. All these considerations led me to the irresistible
conclusion that Jefferson Hope was to be found among the jarveys of the
Metropolis.

conclusion that Jefferson Hope was to be found among the jarveys of the
Metropolis.

5. These observations led to the conclusion that Jefferson Hope, who was suspected of murder, must have been working as a cab driver (jarvey) in London.

3. The role of a cabdriver provides a plausible cover for surveillance activities.

“If he had been one there was no reason to believe that he had ceased
to be. On the contrary, from his point of view, any sudden change would
be likely to draw attention to himself. He would, probably, for a time
at least, continue to perform his duties. There was no reason to
suppose that he was going under an assumed name. Why should he change
his name in a country where no one knew his original one? I therefore
organized my Street Arab detective corps, and sent them systematically
to every cab proprietor in London until they ferreted out the man that
I wanted. How well they succeeded, and how quickly I took advantage of
it, are still fresh in your recollection. The murder of Stangerson was
an incident which was entirely unexpected, but which could hardly in
any case have been prevented. Through it, as you know, I came into
possession of the pills, the existence of which I had already surmised.
You see the whole thing is a chain of logical sequences without a break
or flaw.”

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story. Read it carefully, then complete the structured analysis.

“If he had been one there was no reason to believe that he had ceased
to be. On the contrary, from his point of view, any sudden change would

You see the whole thing is a chain of logical sequences without a break
or flaw.”

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. The individual in question would likely continue to perform his duties if he was still a detective.
2. There was no reason to believe that he had stopped being a detective and adopted an assumed name.
3. Changing one's name would be unnecessary in a country where nobody knew the original name.
4. A Street Arab detective corps was organized and sent to question cab proprietors systematically to find the man of interest.
5. The murder of Stangerson occurred unexpectedly but could not have been prevented.
6. Through this incident, pills were discovered which had already been suspected by the investigator.
7. The entire case is seen as a logical sequence without any breaks or flaws.
1. The detective believes that if someone was a detective, they would likely continue to perform their duties.
2. The detective concludes there's no reason for the individual in question to have stopped being a detective and taken on an assumed name.
3. In London, where nobody knew his original name, changing one’s name would be unnecessary.
4. A team of detectives was dispatched to gather information from cab proprietors about their passengers.
5. The unexpected murder of Stangerson occurred but couldn't have been prevented.
6. The discovery of pills during the investigation confirmed suspicions that had already existed.
7. The detective views all these events as logically connected without any gaps or inconsistencies.
CONCLUSION: The detective deduces through a series of logical inferences that the individual is still likely performing his duties and concludes that the murder and subsequent pill discovery are part of a continuous, unbroken chain of evidence leading to the solution of the case.

“The public,” it said, “have lost a sensational treat through the
sudden death of the man Hope, who was suspected of the murder of Mr.
Enoch Drebber and of Mr. Joseph Stangerson. The details of the case
will probably be never known now, though we are informed upon good
authority that the crime was the result of an old standing and romantic
feud, in which love and Mormonism bore a part. It seems that both the
victims belonged, in their younger days, to the Latter Day Saints, and
Hope, the deceased prisoner, hails also from Salt Lake City. If the
case has had no other effect, it, at least, brings out in the most
striking manner the efficiency of our detective police force, and will
serve as a lesson to all foreigners that they will do wisely to settle
their feuds at home, and not to carry them on to British soil. It is an
open secret that the credit of this smart capture belongs entirely to
the well-known Scotland Yard officials, Messrs. Lestrade and Gregson.
The man was apprehended, it appears, in the rooms of a certain Mr.
Sherlock Holmes, who has himself, as an amateur, shown some talent in
the detective line, and who, with such instructors, may hope in time to
attain to some degree of their skill. It is expected that a testimonial
of some sort will be presented to the two officers as a fitting
recognition of their services.”

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
“The public,” it said, “have lost a sensational treat through the
sudden death of the man Hope, who was suspected of the murder of Mr.
Enoch Drebber and of Mr. Joseph Stangerson. The details of the case
will probably be never known now, though we are informed upon good

The man was apprehended, it appears, in the rooms of a certain Mr.

of some sort will be presented to the two officers as a fitting
recognition of their services.”

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
In retrospect, it was Holmes who first noted the peculiarities in the details of Mr. Hope's case, particularly the connection to Salt Lake City and the mention of a "romantic feud" involving love and Mormonism. At the time, I found the entire affair rather dull and uninteresting, merely noting that yet another sensational story had been squelched by the efficient workings of Scotland Yard. Holmes, however, saw in it an opportunity for his own amateur detective work, revealing not only his keen eye for detail but also his method of seeking out cases where others might see none worth pursuing.

To Sherlock Holmes she is always _the_ woman. I have seldom heard him
mention her under any other name. In his eyes she eclipses and
predominates the whole of her sex. It was not that he felt any emotion
akin to love for Irene Adler. All emotions, and that one particularly,
were abhorrent to his cold, precise but admirably balanced mind. He
was, I take it, the most perfect reasoning and observing machine that
the world has seen, but as a lover he would have placed himself in a
false position. He never spoke of the softer passions, save with a gibe
and a sneer. They were admirable things for the observer—excellent for
drawing the veil from men’s motives and actions. But for the trained
reasoner to admit such intrusions into his own delicate and finely
adjusted temperament was to introduce a distracting factor which might
throw a doubt upon all his mental results. Grit in a sensitive
instrument, or a crack in one of his own high-power lenses, would not
be more disturbing than a strong emotion in a nature such as his. And

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
To Sherlock Holmes she is always _the_ woman. I have seldom heard him
mention her under any other name. In his eyes she eclipses and
predominates the whole of her sex. It was not that he felt any emotion
akin to love for Irene Adler. All emotions, and that one particularly,
were abhorrent to his cold, precise but admirably balanced mind. He
was, I take it, the most perfect reasoning and observing machine that
the world has seen, but as a lover he would have placed himself in a
false position. He never spoke of the softer passions, save with a gibe
and a sneer. They were admirable things for the observer—excellent for
drawing the veil from men’s motives and actions. But for the trained
reasoner to admit such intrusions into his own delicate and finely
adjusted temperament was to introduce a distracting factor which might
throw a doubt upon all his mental results. Grit in a sensitive
instrument, or a crack in one of his own high-power lenses, would not
be more disturbing than a strong emotion in a nature such as his. And
yet there was but one woman to him, and that woman was the late Irene
Adler, of dubious and questionable memory.

WATSON'S ACCOUNT:
At the time, I found Holmes' fascination with Miss Adler quite perplexing; her name was barely mentioned in our cases, yet he spoke of her as if she held a secret that could unravel the complexities of his own mind. It wasn't until later, when I observed him in moments of quiet introspection or deep contemplation, that I truly understood how her case had served as a mirror to his own intellectual and emotional boundaries. Holmes's method was not just about observation and deduction; it also required an understanding of the human psyche, even if he often dismissed such insights as mere distractions.

I had seen little of Holmes lately. My marriage had drifted us away
from each other. My own complete happiness, and the home-centred
interests which rise up around the man who first finds himself master
of his own establishment, were sufficient to absorb all my attention,
while Holmes, who loathed every form of society with his whole Bohemian
soul, remained in our lodgings in Baker Street, buried among his old
books, and alternating from week to week between cocaine and ambition,
the drowsiness of the drug, and the fierce energy of his own keen
nature. He was still, as ever, deeply attracted by the study of crime,
and occupied his immense faculties and extraordinary powers of
observation in following out those clues, and clearing up those
mysteries which had been abandoned as hopeless by the official police.
From time to time I heard some vague account of his doings: of his
summons to Odessa in the case of the Trepoff murder, of his clearing up
of the singular tragedy of the Atkinson brothers at Trincomalee, and
finally of the mission which he had accomplished so delicately and
successfully for the reigning family of Holland. Beyond these signs of
his activity, however, which I merely shared with all the readers of
the daily press, I knew little of my former friend and companion.

One night—it was on the twentieth of March, 1888—I was returning from a
journey to a patient (for I had now returned to civil practice), when
my way led me through Baker Street. As I passed the well-remembered
door, which must always be associated in my mind with my wooing, and
with the dark incidents of the Study in Scarlet, I was seized with a
keen desire to see Holmes again, and to know how he was employing his
extraordinary powers. His rooms were brilliantly lit, and, even as I
looked up, I saw his tall, spare figure pass twice in a dark silhouette
against the blind. He was pacing the room swiftly, eagerly, with his
head sunk upon his chest and his hands clasped behind him. To me, who
knew his every mood and habit, his attitude and manner told their own
story. He was at work again. He had risen out of his drug-created
dreams and was hot upon the scent of some new problem. I rang the bell
and was shown up to the chamber which had formerly been in part my own.

“It is simplicity itself,” said he; “my eyes tell me that on the inside
of your left shoe, just where the firelight strikes it, the leather is
scored by six almost parallel cuts. Obviously they have been caused by
someone who has very carelessly scraped round the edges of the sole in
order to remove crusted mud from it. Hence, you see, my double
deduction that you had been out in vile weather, and that you had a
particularly malignant boot-slitting specimen of the London slavey. As
to your practice, if a gentleman walks into my rooms smelling of
iodoform, with a black mark of nitrate of silver upon his right
forefinger, and a bulge on the right side of his top-hat to show where
he has secreted his stethoscope, I must be dull, indeed, if I do not
pronounce him to be an active member of the medical profession.”

At first, my mind raced with thoughts of the patient, trying to recall his peculiar habits and mannerisms, but Holmes’ focus on minute details of the shoe and hat revealed his superior deductive skills. His ability to see what I missed, the tiny clues that others would overlook, was a testament to his methodical approach and keen observation—traits that made him not just a brilliant detective, but an extraordinary companion.

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by Watson's retrospective account of the same scene.

PASSAGE:
“It is simplicity itself,” said he; “my eyes tell me that on the inside
of your left shoe, just where the firelight strikes it, the leather is
scored by six almost parallel cuts. Obviously they have been caused by
someone who has very carelessly scraped round the edges of the sole in
order to remove crusted mud from it. Hence, you see, my double
deduction that you had been out in vile weather, and that you had a
particularly malignant boot-slitting specimen of the London slavey. As
to your practice, if a gentleman walks into my rooms smelling of
iodoform, with a black mark of nitrate of silver upon his right
forefinger, and a bulge on the right side of his top-hat to show where
he has secreted his stethoscope, I must be dull, indeed, if I do not
pronounce him to be an active member of the medical profession.”

A: From the meticulous examination of the gentleman's footwear, I surmise that the individual had engaged in a strenuous outdoor excursion under adverse conditions, as evidenced by the deliberate clean-up of mud on his shoe. Moreover, the scent of iodoform and the presence of a nitrate of silver mark upon his forefinger suggest that he is well-acquainted with medical procedures, perhaps as a practitioner himself. Finally, the concealed stethoscope within his hat serves as a testament to his clandestine profession, further corroborating my deduction that this gentleman is not merely a casual observer but an active participant in the medical field, operating under the radar of societal expectations.

OBSERVATIONS (what can be directly seen or measured):
1.1. The speaker observes that there are six almost parallel cuts on the inside of the left shoe.
2. These cuts were made by someone scraping around the edges of the sole to remove mud.
3. The leather is scored where the firelight strikes it, indicating the cuts are visible under light.
4. The speaker deduces that the person had been out in bad weather.
5. The speaker infers that the individual had a "malignant boot-slitting specimen of the London slavey" (likely referring to a servant or laborer).
6. The man's presence is smelled of iodoform, suggesting he has recently used medicinal substances.
7. There is a black mark on his right forefinger from nitrate of silver, likely indicating recent use in medical work.
8. His hat has a bulge on the right side where he has hidden his stethoscope.
9. The speaker concludes that this man is an active member of the medical profession.

CONCLUSION: The individual observed by the speaker is a poorly-shod London servant who had been out in bad weather and is also a practicing doctor, as evidenced by the presence of iodoform on him, a mark from nitrate of silver on his finger, and a stethoscope hidden under his hat.

“Peculiar—that is the very word,” said Holmes. “It is not an English

“The name of the maker, no doubt; or his monogram, rather.”

“Not at all. The ‘G’ with the small ‘t’ stands for ‘Gesellschaft,’
which is the German for ‘Company.’ It is a customary contraction like
our ‘Co.’ ‘P,’ of course, stands for ‘Papier.’ Now for the ‘Eg.’ Let us
glance at our Continental Gazetteer.” He took down a heavy brown volume
from his shelves. “Eglow, Eglonitz—here we are, Egria. It is in a
German-speaking country—in Bohemia, not far from Carlsbad. ‘Remarkable
as being the scene of the death of Wallenstein, and for its numerous
glass-factories and paper-mills.’ Ha, ha, my boy, what do you make of
that?” His eyes sparkled, and he sent up a great blue triumphant cloud
from his cigarette.

At three o’clock precisely I was at Baker Street, but Holmes had not
yet returned. The landlady informed me that he had left the house
shortly after eight o’clock in the morning. I sat down beside the fire,
however, with the intention of awaiting him, however long he might be.
I was already deeply interested in his inquiry, for, though it was
surrounded by none of the grim and strange features which were
associated with the two crimes which I have already recorded, still,
the nature of the case and the exalted station of his client gave it a
character of its own. Indeed, apart from the nature of the
investigation which my friend had on hand, there was something in his
masterly grasp of a situation, and his keen, incisive reasoning, which
made it a pleasure to me to study his system of work, and to follow the
quick, subtle methods by which he disentangled the most inextricable
mysteries. So accustomed was I to his invariable success that the very
possibility of his failing had ceased to enter into my head.

“I can’t imagine. I suppose that you have been watching the habits, and

“Oh, she has turned all the men’s heads down in that part. She is the
daintiest thing under a bonnet on this planet. So say the
Serpentine-mews, to a man. She lives quietly, sings at concerts, drives
out at five every day, and returns at seven sharp for dinner. Seldom
goes out at other times, except when she sings. Has only one male
visitor, but a good deal of him. He is dark, handsome, and dashing,
never calls less than once a day, and often twice. He is a Mr. Godfrey
Norton, of the Inner Temple. See the advantages of a cabman as a
confidant. They had driven him home a dozen times from Serpentine-mews,
and knew all about him. When I had listened to all they had to tell, I
began to walk up and down near Briony Lodge once more, and to think
over my plan of campaign.

widened the field of my inquiry. I fear that I bore you with these
details, but I have to let you see my little difficulties, if you are
to understand the situation.”

“You see,” remarked Holmes, as we paced to and fro in front of the
house, “this marriage rather simplifies matters. The photograph becomes
a double-edged weapon now. The chances are that she would be as averse
to its being seen by Mr. Godfrey Norton, as our client is to its coming
to the eyes of his princess. Now the question is, Where are we to find
the photograph?”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

“It is most unlikely that she carries it about with her. It is cabinet
size. Too large for easy concealment about a woman’s dress. She knows
that the King is capable of having her waylaid and searched. Two
attempts of the sort have already been made. We may take it, then, that
she does not carry it about with her.”

Below is a passage from a Victorian detective story, followed by a question and answer based on it.

PASSAGE:
“It is most unlikely that she carries it about with her. It is cabinet
size. Too large for easy concealment about a woman’s dress. She knows
that the King is capable of having her waylaid and searched. Two
attempts of the sort have already been made. We may take it, then, that
she does not carry it about with her.”

Q: Given the observations described above, what does the detective conclude, and how does he arrive at that conclusion?

A: Given that the object in question is of cabinet size—far too cumbersome for concealment within a woman’s attire—the likelihood of its daily carriage by said lady can be dismissed outright. Furthermore, the knowledge that His Majesty has orchestrated at least two attempts to intercept and search her, thereby ensuring she cannot carry such an item with her, compels us to infer with considerable certainty that our subject does not possess the object in her immediate possession, nor is it likely she carries it upon her person or within her custody. Thus, any future efforts to recover this cabinet-sized artifact must focus elsewhere, perhaps on securing a more permanent and secure storage facility under the King’s control or a trusted chamber where such an item could be concealed without attracting undue suspicion.

“Her banker or her lawyer. There is that double possibility. But I am
inclined to think neither. Women are naturally secretive, and they like
to do their own secreting. Why should she hand it over to anyone else?
She could trust her own guardianship, but she could not tell what
indirect or political influence might be brought to bear upon a
business man. Besides, remember that she had resolved to use it within
a few days. It must be where she can lay her hands upon it. It must be
in her own house.”
